lesson_title: Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.front: What is a circular reference with std::shared_ptr and why is it a problem?
cards.back: When two objects have shared_ptrs to each other, their reference counts never reach zero, causing a memory leak. Neither can be deleted because each keeps the other alive.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.question: Why does this code cause a memory leak?
cards.answer: Circular reference. n1 owns n2, and n2 owns n1. Reference counts stay at 2, so neither is ever deleted.
cards.explanation: When n1 and n2 go out of scope, each still has a reference count of 1 due to the circular reference, preventing deletion.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.text: std::weak_ptr is a smart pointer that {{c1::observes}} but does not {{c2::own}} a shared_ptr's resource. It doesn't affect the {{c3::reference count}}.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.text: Create a weak_ptr from a shared_ptr: {{c1::std::weak_ptr<Type> wptr = sptr;}}
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.front: How do you access the object pointed to by a std::weak_ptr?
cards.back:
You can't access it directly. You must first convert it to a shared_ptr using lock():
auto sptr = wptr.lock();
Then check if sptr is null before using it.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.text: Call {{c1::wptr.lock()}} to create a shared_ptr from a weak_ptr. If the object was deleted, lock() returns {{c2::nullptr}}.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.question: How do you safely use a weak_ptr?
cards.code:
std::weak_ptr<int> wptr = sptr;
// How to use wptr?
cards.answer:
if (auto sptr = wptr.lock()) {
  // use sptr safely
  std::cout << *sptr;
} else {
  // object was deleted
}
cards.explanation: lock() returns a shared_ptr if the object still exists, or nullptr if it was deleted. Always check before use.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.concept: Using weak_ptr to break circular references
cards.explanation:
To break circular references, make one direction use weak_ptr:
  • Parent has shared_ptr to child (owns)
  • Child has weak_ptr to parent (observes)
This allows proper cleanup when the parent is destroyed.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.text: Call {{c1::wptr.expired()}} to check if the weak_ptr's object has been deleted. Returns {{c2::true}} if deleted, {{c3::false}} if still valid.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.front: What are the main use cases for std::weak_ptr?
cards.back:
Use std::weak_ptr for:
  • Breaking circular references
  • Caching (observer doesn't own)
  • Observer pattern (observers don't own subject)
  • Back-pointers in tree structures (child to parent)
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: When sptr goes out of scope, the int is deleted because weak_ptr doesn't contribute to reference count. wptr.expired() returns true.
cards.source: 22.7 - Circular dependency issues with std::shared_ptr, and std::weak_ptr

cards.explanation:
Best practice for parent-child relationships:
  • Parent owns children: std::shared_ptr or std::unique_ptr
  • Child references parent: std::weak_ptr
  • Siblings: shared_ptr if shared ownership needed
This prevents circular references and ensures proper cleanup.
examples: // Parent owns child, child observes parent
